stem: A 58-year-old man with metastatic lung cancer presents to the OR for a subxiphoid pericardial window. He is dyspneic, sitting upright, and anxious. Vitals: HR 128/min, BP 92/74 mmHg (pulsus paradoxus 18 mmHg), JVP elevated, and bedside echo shows a large circumferential effusion with right atrial systolic collapse and a plethoric IVC. The surgical team requests general endotracheal anesthesia. Which anesthetic plan is most appropriate to avoid hemodynamic collapse at induction?

choices.text: Maintain spontaneous ventilation, drain the effusion under local infiltration with minimal sedation, and induce general anesthesia only after pericardial decompression if needed
choices.rationale: Correct: deferring positive-pressure ventilation and deep anesthesia until the tamponade is relieved preserves preload, heart rate, and contractility, which is the core 'full, fast, tight' management of tamponade.

choices.text: Perform a rapid-sequence induction with high-dose propofol and rocuronium, then initiate controlled positive-pressure ventilation
choices.rationale: Incorrect: propofol-induced venodilation and myocardial depression combined with the increased intrathoracic pressure of positive-pressure ventilation can precipitate cardiac arrest in a tamponade physiology that depends on preload.

choices.text: Induce with a generous dose of volatile anesthetic by mask before securing the airway to blunt sympathetic tone
choices.rationale: Incorrect: volatile agents cause dose-dependent myocardial depression and vasodilation, abolishing the compensatory sympathetic tone the patient depends on to maintain cardiac output.

choices.text: Aggressively diurese with furosemide preoperatively to reduce the size of the effusion before induction
choices.rationale: Incorrect: diuresis lowers preload, which is exactly the compensatory mechanism sustaining stroke volume in tamponade, and would worsen rather than improve hemodynamics.

choices.text: Pretreat with a beta-blocker to control the heart rate to a normal range before proceeding with standard induction
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the tachycardia is a compensatory response that preserves cardiac output when stroke volume is fixed, so slowing the heart rate will reduce output and can be catastrophic.

explanation: Cardiac tamponade produces a preload-dependent, fixed-stroke-volume state in which compensatory tachycardia and elevated sympathetic tone maintain output. Anesthetic management follows 'full, fast, tight': maintain preload, keep the heart rate up, and preserve afterload/contractility, while avoiding venodilators, myocardial depressants, and positive-pressure ventilation until the pericardium is decompressed. Whenever feasible, drainage under local anesthesia with spontaneous ventilation is preferred, and ketamine is a favored induction agent because it preserves sympathetic tone.
subtopic: Cardiac tamponade — induction hemodynamics and pericardial decompression

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Stem is unambiguous cardiac tamponade (large circumferential effusion, RA collapse, plethoric IVC, tachycardia, hypotension, pulsus paradoxus 18 mmHg). Keyed answer (index 0) — maintain spontaneous ventilation, drain under local infiltration with minimal sedation, defer GA/PPV until decompression — is the single best, universally taught approach ("full, fast, tight"; avoid venodilators, myocardial depressants, and positive-pressure ventilation until the pericardium is decompressed). All four distractors are genuinely inferior and the rationales are accurate: RSI propofol+PPV (preload loss + raised intrathoracic pressure → collapse), volatile mask induction (myocardial depression/vasodilation, abolishes compensatory sympathetic tone), aggressive diuresis (lowers the preload that sustains stroke volume), and beta-blockade (slows the compensatory tachycardia with a fixed stroke volume). Explanation correctly notes ketamine is a favored induction agent. Only trivial nuance: RA collapse is classically late-diastolic/early-systolic, but "systolic collapse" is acceptable shorthand and does not affect the answer. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia URL resolves to a "Cardiac Tamponade" page that directly covers anesthetic management — induction risk, "fast, full, tight," avoiding positive-pressure ventilation, ketamine, and pericardial drainage. Citation is valid and on-topic. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 67-year-old woman with insulin-dependent diabetes (on NPH insulin) is separating from cardiopulmonary bypass after a CABG. Heparin is reversed with a slow protamine infusion. About two minutes into the dose, her systolic pulmonary artery pressure abruptly rises from 30 to 70 mmHg, the right ventricle becomes distended and hypokinetic on TEE, systemic BP falls to 60/40 mmHg, and CVP climbs sharply. There is no rash or bronchospasm. Which statement best characterizes this reaction and its management?

choices.text: This is a catastrophic protamine reaction from thromboxane-mediated pulmonary vasoconstriction; stop the protamine, support the right ventricle, and consider a pulmonary vasodilator such as inhaled prostacyclin or nitric oxide
choices.rationale: Correct: the abrupt rise in PA pressure with acute RV failure and systemic hypotension is the classic type III thromboxane-mediated pulmonary vasoconstrictive protamine reaction, managed by halting the drug, supporting the RV, and reducing pulmonary vascular resistance.

choices.text: This is simple protamine-induced systemic vasodilation from histamine and nitric oxide; slow the infusion and give a vasopressor and it will resolve without further concern
choices.rationale: Incorrect: simple vasodilatory hypotension is not accompanied by a dramatic rise in pulmonary artery pressure and acute RV distension, which point to pulmonary vasoconstriction rather than systemic vasodilation.

choices.text: This represents residual heparin effect; the correct action is to administer an additional large bolus of protamine to complete reversal
choices.rationale: Incorrect: residual heparin causes bleeding, not acute pulmonary hypertension, and giving more protamine would worsen a protamine reaction and can itself precipitate the pulmonary vasoconstrictive response.

choices.text: Her NPH insulin use is irrelevant to protamine risk, and the picture is most consistent with acute graft thrombosis
choices.rationale: Incorrect: NPH insulin contains protamine and is a recognized risk factor for protamine reactions, and graft thrombosis would not produce an abrupt global rise in PA pressure timed precisely to the protamine dose.

choices.text: The first-line treatment is immediate re-heparinization and return to full cardiopulmonary bypass before any other intervention
choices.rationale: Incorrect: while re-establishing bypass may rescue refractory cases, the immediate first steps are to stop the protamine and support the RV with pulmonary vasodilators and inotropes, not to reflexively re-heparinize.

explanation: Protamine reactions are classically grouped into systemic hypotension from histamine/nitric oxide release (rate-related), true anaphylaxis, and the catastrophic thromboxane-mediated pulmonary vasoconstrictive reaction. The third type causes an abrupt surge in pulmonary vascular resistance with acute right ventricular failure and systemic hypotension, and is treated by stopping the protamine, supporting the RV, and lowering PVR with agents such as inhaled prostacyclin or nitric oxide. Patients with prior protamine exposure, fish allergy, vasectomy, or NPH (protamine-containing) insulin are at elevated risk.
subtopic: Protamine reactions and pulmonary vasoconstriction after cardiopulmonary bypass

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 0) is unambiguously the single best answer. The vignette is the textbook presentation of the catastrophic thromboxane-mediated pulmonary vasoconstrictive protamine reaction: abrupt PA pressure surge (30→70 mmHg), acute RV distension/hypokinesis on TEE, systemic hypotension, rising CVP, timed to protamine, with no rash/bronchospasm (which argues against anaphylaxis). Management is correct and current: stop protamine, support the RV with inotropes, and lower PVR with inhaled pulmonary vasodilators (inhaled nitric oxide or prostacyclin/epoprostenol). All four distractors are genuinely inferior: (1) histamine/NO systemic vasodilation does not cause acute pulmonary hypertension or RV failure; (2) residual heparin causes bleeding, not acute PHTN, and more protamine worsens the reaction; (3) NPH insulin is protamine-containing and IS a recognized risk factor, and graft thrombosis would not produce an abrupt global PA-pressure surge timed to the dose; (4) re-heparinization/return to CPB is a rescue maneuver for refractory cases, not the immediate first step. Rationales and explanation are accurate. The "Type III" label is a defensible, commonly cited classification and does not create ambiguity since only one option matches the hemodynamics. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK547753/ resolves to "Protamine — StatPearls (NCBI Bookshelf)" and directly supports the content, explicitly discussing thromboxane A2-mediated pulmonary hypertension as a catastrophic reaction (PVR up to 10x), NPH insulin as a risk factor, and prostacyclin infusion as treatment. Citation is accurate and on-topic.

stem: A 74-year-old pacemaker-dependent man with complete heart block and a dual-chamber pacemaker is scheduled for an open umbilical hernia repair using monopolar electrocautery. His interrogation report lists the device as having an asynchronous magnet response. Intraoperatively, you anticipate electromagnetic interference from cautery near the chest. Which perioperative strategy is most appropriate to prevent inappropriate pacing inhibition during the case?

choices.text: Reprogram the device to an asynchronous mode (e.g., DOO) preoperatively, or apply a magnet intraoperatively to force asynchronous pacing, and keep the dispersive (grounding) pad positioned so the cautery current path is directed away from the generator
choices.rationale: Correct: in a pacemaker-dependent patient, converting the device to asynchronous pacing (by reprogramming or magnet) prevents EMI-induced oversensing and pacing inhibition, and proper grounding-pad placement minimizes current crossing the device.

choices.text: Take no special precautions because monopolar cautery below the umbilicus rarely causes clinically significant interference in modern devices
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the surgical site is at/above the umbilicus and near the chest, which is precisely where EMI risk is highest, so omitting precautions risks pacing inhibition and asystole in a dependent patient.

choices.text: Rely on the device's built-in noise-reversion algorithm and simply disable the rate-response feature to handle the interference
choices.rationale: Incorrect: disabling rate response alone does not guarantee asynchronous pacing during sustained EMI, and noise reversion is not a reliable substitute for deliberately programming or magnet-converting a dependent patient to asynchronous mode.

choices.text: Place an external transcutaneous pacing system on standby but make no change to the implanted device, since the magnet response is unpredictable in all pacemakers
choices.rationale: Incorrect: although backup external pacing is prudent, this device's interrogation specifically documents an asynchronous magnet response, so the magnet is a reliable intraoperative tool rather than something to avoid.

choices.text: Switch the implanted device into ICD therapy-off mode to prevent inappropriate shocks from the cautery
choices.rationale: Incorrect: this is a pacemaker without defibrillator function, so there are no anti-tachycardia shocks to disable, and the relevant concern is pacing inhibition rather than inappropriate shocks.

explanation: In a pacemaker-dependent patient, monopolar electrocautery near the generator can be oversensed as intrinsic activity and inhibit pacing, producing bradycardia or asystole. The protective strategy is to make pacing asynchronous, either by preoperative reprogramming to a mode such as DOO or by applying a magnet to a device with a documented asynchronous magnet response, together with bipolar/ultrasonic cautery when possible and grounding-pad placement that keeps the current path away from the generator. A magnet over a pacemaker triggers asynchronous pacing, whereas over an ICD it only suspends anti-tachycardia therapies and does not change the pacing mode.
subtopic: Cardiac implantable electronic devices — EMI, magnet response, and pacemaker dependence
reference_title: Cardiac Implantable Electronic Devices: Pacemakers — OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason:
CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 0) is the single best and unambiguous choice. In a pacemaker-dependent patient undergoing monopolar electrocautery, rendering the device asynchronous (reprogram to DOO, or apply a magnet given the documented asynchronous magnet response) to prevent oversensing/pacing inhibition, plus dispersive-pad placement that keeps the ESU current path away from the generator, is standard ASA/HRS perioperative CIED management. All distractors are genuinely inferior: B (no precautions) is unsafe in a dependent patient; C (noise reversion + disabling rate response) is not a reliable substitute for asynchronous pacing; D (external pacing only, claiming magnet is unreliable) is wrong because the interrogation documents a reliable asynchronous magnet response; E (ICD therapy-off) is irrelevant since this is a pacemaker with no shocks to disable. The explanation, including the magnet-over-pacemaker (asynchronous pacing) vs magnet-over-ICD (suspends anti-tachycardia therapy only, no pacing-mode change) contrast, is accurate and current.

Minor blemish (not disqualifying): the stem/choice-B describe the cautery as 'near the chest' and 'at/above the umbilicus,' but an umbilical hernia repair is at the umbilicus (mid-abdomen), not near the chest. This is internal-consistency sloppiness; it does not change the correct answer or create ambiguity, because precautions are warranted in a dependent patient regardless of cautery location.

REFERENCE: WebFetch confirms the OpenAnesthesia URL resolves with matching title 'Cardiac Implantable Electronic Devices: Pacemakers' and directly covers pacemakers, EMI/electrocautery, magnet-induced asynchronous pacing, pacemaker dependence (risk of bradycardia/asystole), dispersive-pad placement away from the generator, and perioperative management aligned with ASA guidance. Citation is valid and on-topic.

stem: A 4-year-old, 16-kg boy is undergoing examination under anesthesia and cystoscopy. He received a topical anesthetic spray to the urethra and an intravenous dose of a local anesthetic-containing agent for a regional block. About 30 minutes into the case he becomes dusky and the pulse oximeter reads 84% despite an FiO2 of 1.0 and a normal, well-perfused plethysmographic waveform. Breath sounds are clear and equal, the capnogram is normal with an ETCO2 of 34 mmHg, and peak airway pressures are unchanged. An arterial blood gas drawn at this time shows PaO2 230 mmHg with a calculated SaO2 of 99%. The SpO2 does not change when FiO2 is reduced to 0.5. Which mechanism best explains the discrepancy between the SpO2 and the PaO2?

choices.text: Methemoglobin absorbs red (660 nm) and infrared (940 nm) light nearly equally, driving the absorption ratio toward 1:1 and fixing the displayed SpO2 near 85%
choices.rationale: Correct: methemoglobinemia (here from a benzocaine/prilocaine-type agent) produces equal 660/940 nm absorption, an R value of ~1.0, and a characteristic plateau of SpO2 around 85% regardless of true SaO2.

choices.text: Carboxyhemoglobin is being read as oxyhemoglobin, which causes the pulse oximeter to overestimate true saturation
choices.rationale: Incorrect: carboxyhemoglobin absorbs like oxyhemoglobin and falsely raises (not lowers) SpO2 toward normal, the opposite of this low, fixed reading.

choices.text: A right-to-left intracardiac shunt is producing true hypoxemia that the high PaO2 fails to capture
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a real shunt would lower the measured PaO2 and SaO2, but this child's PaO2 is 230 mmHg with a co-oximetered SaO2 of 99%.

choices.text: Motion artifact and low perfusion are corrupting the plethysmographic signal
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the stem specifies a normal, well-perfused waveform, which excludes motion or low-perfusion artifact as the cause.

choices.text: Venous pulsation from a venous congestion artifact is being sampled as arterial blood
choices.rationale: Incorrect: venous pulsation artifact is uncommon, would not produce a precisely fixed ~85% reading, and does not fit a normal arterial waveform with a markedly elevated PaO2.

explanation: Methemoglobin has nearly identical extinction coefficients at the two pulse-oximeter wavelengths (660 nm red and 940 nm infrared), so as the metHb fraction rises the red/infrared absorption ratio is driven toward 1.0, which the device translates to a fixed SpO2 of roughly 85% independent of the true SaO2. A wide saturation gap (low fixed SpO2 with a high PaO2 and normal co-oximetered SaO2) after a known oxidizing agent such as benzocaine or prilocaine is the classic presentation. Diagnosis requires multi-wavelength co-oximetry, and treatment is methylene blue 1-2 mg/kg IV (avoid in G6PD deficiency, where ascorbic acid is used instead).
subtopic: Pulse oximetry physics and dyshemoglobinemias (methemoglobinemia)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (A) is the single best answer and is unambiguous. The stem describes a classic methemoglobinemia presentation: a saturation gap (fixed low SpO2 ~84% with PaO2 230 mmHg and co-oximetered SaO2 99%), clear breath sounds/normal capnogram excluding respiratory causes, a well-perfused plethysmographic waveform excluding artifact, and exposure to two oxidizing agents (benzocaine-type topical spray + prilocaine-type IV/regional agent). MetHb has nearly equal extinction coefficients at 660 nm and 940 nm, driving the R ratio toward ~1.0, which the oximeter calibration maps to a fixed SpO2 plateau near 85% independent of true SaO2 — physics is accurately stated. The fixed reading despite reducing FiO2 from 1.0 to 0.5 reinforces this. All distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly refuted: COHb (B) falsely RAISES SpO2 (opposite direction); a right-to-left shunt (C) would lower PaO2/SaO2, contradicted by PaO2 230 and SaO2 99%; motion/low perfusion (D) is excluded by the stated normal waveform; venous pulsation (E) does not produce a precisely fixed ~85% reading. Treatment note (methylene blue 1-2 mg/kg IV, avoid in G6PD where ascorbic acid is used) is accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/methemoglobinemia/ resolves successfully and is squarely on-topic — it explicitly states MetHb has equal red/infrared absorption coefficients yielding a falsely low ~85% SpO2, identifies benzocaine (dose-independent) and prilocaine (dose-dependent) as culprits, and gives methylene blue 1-2 mg/kg with the G6PD/vitamin C caveat. Both clinical content and citation are sound.

stem: An anesthesia team arrives early on a Monday for the first case in an outpatient surgery center; an inhalational induction and maintenance with desflurane via a circle system at a fresh gas flow of 1 L/min is planned. The machine's vaporizer and circuit were last used the previous Thursday, and high oxygen flows were inadvertently left running through the absorber over the weekend. Shortly after desflurane is started, the CO2 absorbent canister feels noticeably warm and the patient's pulse oximetry reads 99% while a co-oximetry sample later shows an elevated carboxyhemoglobin level. Which combination of conditions is the leading cause of the carbon monoxide generation in this circuit?

choices.text: Desiccated absorbent containing strong bases (NaOH/KOH) degrading desflurane, worsened by the high heat and low fresh gas flow
choices.rationale: Correct: dry absorbent containing sodium and potassium hydroxide degrades desflurane (and enflurane/isoflurane) to carbon monoxide, and prolonged high-flow gas over a weekend is the classic desiccating setup, while low flow and an exothermic canister further concentrate CO.

choices.text: Fully hydrated soda lime reacting with sevoflurane to form Compound A
choices.rationale: Incorrect: Compound A is a nephrotoxin (not carbon monoxide), forms with sevoflurane, and is associated with low-flow conditions in normally hydrated absorbent rather than CO production.

choices.text: Adequately moist absorbent (about 15% water) reacting normally to absorb CO2 with no abnormal byproducts
choices.rationale: Incorrect: absorbent with normal water content (roughly 4.8% or more) generates essentially no CO, so a properly hydrated canister would not explain the elevated carboxyhemoglobin.

choices.text: A faulty unidirectional valve causing rebreathing of exhaled carbon monoxide from the patient
choices.rationale: Incorrect: patients do not exhale clinically significant CO, and a stuck valve causes rebreathing of CO2, not de novo carbon monoxide formation.

choices.text: Calcium hydroxide lime free of strong alkali hydroxides accelerating desflurane degradation to CO
choices.rationale: Incorrect: removing NaOH/KOH (as in calcium hydroxide lime) markedly reduces, rather than accelerates, both CO and Compound A formation.

explanation: Carbon monoxide is produced when volatile anesthetics, especially desflurane, enflurane, and isoflurane, are degraded by desiccated CO2 absorbent that contains the strong bases sodium and potassium hydroxide. Desiccation classically occurs when high fresh gas flows are left running through the absorber over a weekend, and the reaction is exothermic, so an unexpectedly warm canister is a warning sign. Prevention includes turning off gas flows when machines are idle, replacing absorbent of uncertain dryness, and using bases without NaOH/KOH (calcium hydroxide lime), which also minimizes sevoflurane degradation to Compound A.
subtopic: Anesthesia delivery equipment: circle system CO2 absorbent chemistry (carbon monoxide production)
reference_title: Carbon Dioxide Absorbent Desiccation Safety Conference Convened by APSF - Anesthesia Patient Safety Foundation

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 0) is the single best answer and is unambiguous. CO production by desiccated CO2 absorbent containing strong bases (NaOH/KOH) degrading volatile anesthetics is the classic, well-established mechanism; the rank order desflurane>=enflurane>isoflurane (sevoflurane minimal CO) is correct; the Monday-morning first-case after high gas flows left running over a weekend is the textbook desiccation scenario; the exothermic reaction (warm canister) is a real warning sign; low fresh gas flow concentrates CO in the circuit. All distractors are genuinely inferior: (B) Compound A is a sevoflurane-derived nephrotoxin, not CO; (C) normally hydrated absorbent yields negligible CO; (D) patients do not exhale clinically significant CO and a faulty valve causes CO2 rebreathing, not de novo CO; (E) calcium hydroxide lime lacking NaOH/KOH reduces (not accelerates) CO. Stem is internally consistent (high-flow desiccation over weekend, low-flow concentration during case). REFERENCE: reference_url returns HTTP 403, but this is Cloudflare bot protection ('Just a moment...' challenge), not a 404. Independent verification (DuckDuckGo, Semantic Scholar, a PANA citation list) confirms the article genuinely exists at the exact cited URL: Olympio MA, 'Carbon Dioxide Absorbent Desiccation Safety Conference Convened by APSF,' APSF Newsletter 2005;20(2). It is precisely on-topic for CO2 absorbent desiccation/CO safety. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: An 82-year-old woman with mild baseline cognitive impairment, hypertension, and chronic low back pain is admitted after a fall for surgical fixation of an intertrochanteric hip fracture. She is taking lisinopril and acetaminophen. The orthopedic team requests an anesthetic plan that minimizes her risk of postoperative delirium. As the CAA reviewing her perioperative medications and plan, which of the following interventions is MOST likely to reduce her risk of postoperative delirium?

choices.text: Premedicate with intravenous midazolam 2 mg before transport to the operating room to reduce anxiety
choices.rationale: Incorrect: benzodiazepines are on the AGS Beers list of potentially inappropriate medications and are an independent precipitant of postoperative delirium in older adults, so routine premedication increases rather than reduces her risk.

choices.text: Substitute meperidine for other opioids to provide superior analgesia with fewer cognitive effects
choices.rationale: Incorrect: meperidine's anticholinergic activity and the CNS-active metabolite normeperidine make it the opioid most strongly associated with delirium, so it is specifically discouraged in the elderly.

choices.text: Use a multimodal, opioid-sparing analgesic strategy that includes a regional nerve block such as a fascia iliaca block
choices.rationale: Correct: multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia including regional blocks reduces opioid exposure and improves pain control, both of which are evidence-supported strategies for lowering postoperative delirium risk in hip fracture patients.

choices.text: Add scopolamine transdermal patch preoperatively to prevent postoperative nausea and vomiting
choices.rationale: Incorrect: scopolamine is a centrally acting anticholinergic on the Beers list and is a recognized precipitant of delirium, so it should be avoided in this high-risk patient.

choices.text: Plan for deep sedation with continuous diphenhydramine infusion to keep the patient comfortable and still
choices.rationale: Incorrect: diphenhydramine is a sedating anticholinergic antihistamine that worsens delirium risk, and deliberately deep sedation offers no protective benefit while compounding anticholinergic burden.

explanation: Postoperative delirium prevention in older surgical patients centers on minimizing precipitating factors, especially deliriogenic medications. Benzodiazepines, anticholinergics (scopolamine, diphenhydramine), and meperidine all appear on the AGS Beers Criteria and should be avoided. Multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia incorporating regional techniques such as a fascia iliaca block is one of the better-supported interventions for reducing both pain and the opioid load that drives delirium in hip fracture patients.
subtopic: Postoperative delirium - deliriogenic medications and prevention
reference_title: Postoperative Delirium in Aging Patients - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 2 - multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia with a fascia iliaca/regional block) is the single best answer for reducing postoperative delirium risk in a hip fracture patient; regional nerve blocks for hip fracture analgesia reduce opioid load and are guideline-endorsed and evidence-supported. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and accurately explained: midazolam (benzodiazepine, Beers-listed, independent delirium precipitant), meperidine (anticholinergic + neurotoxic normeperidine metabolite, the opioid most associated with delirium), scopolamine (central anticholinergic, Beers-listed), and diphenhydramine infusion plus deep sedation (anticholinergic antihistamine + deliberate deep sedation, both deliriogenic). The explanation is accurate and current. No ambiguity: the correct choice is best by a wide margin. (Note: the reference says evidence is insufficient to recommend regional/neuraxial as the PRIMARY anesthetic, but the stem asks about regional block within multimodal ANALGESIA, a distinct and well-supported intervention - no contradiction.) REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/postoperative-delirium-in-aging-patients/ resolves to substantive, on-topic OpenAnesthesia/IARS content covering POD, Beers Criteria deliriogenic medications (benzodiazepines, scopolamine, anticholinergics), and multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia - directly supporting the question. Citation is valid.

stem: A 78-year-old man (70 kg) presents for transurethral resection of a bladder tumor under general anesthesia with sevoflurane. He has well-controlled hypertension and no other comorbidities. A resident proposes titrating the sevoflurane to the same end-tidal MAC that would be targeted in a healthy 30-year-old. Compared with that younger patient, which statement BEST describes the expected pharmacology of inhaled and intravenous anesthetics in this older man?

choices.text: Minimum alveolar concentration is unchanged with age, so identical end-tidal sevoflurane concentrations are appropriate
choices.rationale: Incorrect: MAC is not age-independent; it declines progressively with aging, so matching a younger patient's end-tidal target risks relative overdose.

choices.text: Minimum alveolar concentration decreases approximately 6% per decade after age 40, so a lower end-tidal concentration achieves the same anesthetic depth
choices.rationale: Correct: MAC falls roughly 6% per decade beyond age 40, reflecting increased CNS sensitivity, so the older patient needs a meaningfully lower end-tidal sevoflurane concentration.

choices.text: Minimum alveolar concentration increases with age because reduced cardiac output speeds alveolar wash-in
choices.rationale: Incorrect: MAC decreases rather than increases with age, and any kinetic effect of low cardiac output on wash-in does not change the intrinsic dose requirement.

choices.text: Propofol induction doses should be increased because a larger volume of distribution lowers peak plasma concentration in the elderly
choices.rationale: Incorrect: elderly patients require reduced propofol doses owing to heightened pharmacodynamic sensitivity and reduced clearance, not increased doses.

choices.text: Emergence from a prolonged sevoflurane anesthetic is typically faster in the elderly because of decreased adipose tissue
choices.rationale: Incorrect: older adults have increased fat as a fraction of body mass and reduced clearance, which tends to prolong rather than accelerate emergence after prolonged exposure.

explanation: Anesthetic requirements decline with age. MAC for volatile agents decreases by approximately 6% per decade after roughly age 40, reflecting increased central nervous system sensitivity, so older patients reach an equivalent depth at lower end-titled concentrations. Intravenous agents such as propofol likewise require dose reduction due to greater pharmacodynamic sensitivity and reduced clearance, and increased body fat with reduced clearance can prolong emergence after prolonged exposure.
subtopic: Age-related pharmacology of inhaled and IV anesthetics (MAC)
reference_title: Pharmacological Changes with Aging: Inhalational and Intravenous Anesthetics - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 1) is the single best answer. MAC declines ~6% per decade with aging (a well-established figure), reflecting increased CNS sensitivity, so the 78-year-old needs a meaningfully lower end-tidal sevoflurane than the 30-year-old. All distractors are genuinely inferior: MAC is not age-independent (index 0 false); MAC decreases rather than increases (index 2 false); propofol doses must be REDUCED in the elderly due to heightened pharmacodynamic sensitivity and reduced clearance, not increased (index 3 false); and elderly patients have an INCREASED fat fraction plus reduced clearance, which prolongs rather than speeds emergence after prolonged exposure (index 4 false). The rationales are accurate. Two minor blemishes that do NOT affect correctness or create ambiguity: (1) the keyed text anchors the decline 'after age 40' whereas the classic curve (and the reference) describe ~6%/decade decline after roughly age 1; for this 78-vs-30 comparison the direction and magnitude conclusion is unchanged. (2) cosmetic typos in the explanation ('end-titled' should be 'end-tidal'). REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia URL resolves successfully (not a 404) and is squarely on-topic — it explicitly states 'MAC decreases by about 6% per decade' and gives elderly propofol dose-reduction figures (bolus reduced 40-50%, maintenance 30-50%), directly supporting the keyed answer and distractor rationales.

stem: An 84-year-old, 52-kg woman with sarcopenia undergoes a 3-hour open hemicolectomy under general anesthesia in an operating room kept at 19°C. No active warming was used during the first hour. Forty-five minutes after incision her core (esophageal) temperature is 35.2°C, down from 36.6°C at induction. She is normotensive and adequately anesthetized. Which mechanism BEST explains the largest share of this early intraoperative core temperature drop, and what is the most appropriate intervention?

choices.text: Increased metabolic heat production from surgical stress; treat by deepening anesthesia to reduce shivering
choices.rationale: Incorrect: general anesthesia lowers metabolic heat production and abolishes the shivering response, so this is neither the mechanism nor an effective remedy.

choices.text: Anesthesia-induced core-to-peripheral redistribution of heat; mitigate it with prewarming and active forced-air warming
choices.rationale: Correct: the rapid first-hour drop is driven mainly by vasodilation-induced redistribution of heat from core to periphery, which is best blunted by preoperative prewarming and intraoperative forced-air warming.

choices.text: Evaporative water loss from the open abdomen, which accounts for the majority of perioperative heat loss; treat by covering the bowel with dry gauze
choices.rationale: Incorrect: evaporative loss contributes but is not the dominant mechanism of the early phase, and dry gauze is not the principal warming strategy.

choices.text: Cold intravenous fluids lowering blood temperature; treat exclusively by switching to a lower fresh gas flow
choices.rationale: Incorrect: unwarmed fluids add to heat loss but are a minor early contributor, and reducing fresh gas flow alone does not address redistribution.

choices.text: Age-related fever suppression masking sepsis; treat with broad-spectrum antibiotics and a fluid bolus
choices.rationale: Incorrect: this is intraoperative hypothermia, not infection, so an antibiotic and fluid bolus do not address the thermoregulatory mechanism at play.

explanation: The characteristic 1-1.5°C fall in core temperature during the first hour of general anesthesia is driven predominantly by redistribution of heat from the warm core to the cooler periphery as anesthetic-induced vasodilation lowers the vasoconstriction threshold. Elderly patients with sarcopenia, blunted autonomic responses, and reduced thermogenic reserve are especially vulnerable, and anesthetics further lower vasoconstriction and shivering thresholds. Prewarming before induction plus intraoperative forced-air warming is the most effective way to attenuate this redistribution hypothermia and its downstream risks of cardiac events, surgical site infection, and coagulopathy.
subtopic: Perioperative thermoregulation and hypothermia in the elderly
reference_title: Cold temperatures, hot risks: perioperative hypothermia in geriatric patients - a narrative review (PMC)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer B is the single best answer and is unambiguous. The ~1.4°C core temperature fall within the first hour of GA in this elderly, sarcopenic patient is the textbook presentation of anesthesia-induced core-to-peripheral heat redistribution from vasodilation/lowered vasoconstriction threshold (Sessler), correctly treated with prewarming plus forced-air warming. All distractors are genuinely inferior: (A) GA lowers metabolic heat production and abolishes shivering, so 'deepen anesthesia to reduce shivering' is wrong on both mechanism and remedy; (C) evaporative loss is a contributor but not dominant in the early phase and dry gauze is not the principal strategy; (D) cold IV fluids are a minor early contributor and reducing fresh gas flow alone is inadequate; (E) this is hypothermia, not masked sepsis, so antibiotics/fluids are irrelevant. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the reference_url resolves to a real, on-topic article — 'Cold temperatures, hot risks: perioperative hypothermia in geriatric patients - a narrative review,' Anesthesia and Pain Medicine (Seoul) 2025 — which explicitly attributes ~81% of the initial temperature drop to redistribution and endorses prewarming and forced-air warming. Citation is valid and directly supportive. Both clinical and reference dimensions are sound.
